field guide

Child, Adolescent & Family Health

🔍
Clinical Practice
Community
Advanced Practice
Section 01
Child Health & Development
Scope ▶

This section covers child health surveillance, developmental screening, and growth monitoring as delivered within Little Grove's Child Health and Development Check (CHDC) program in ECEC settings. Resources span the full 0–8 year age range and align with Flying Start reporting requirements and the OECD minimum dataset.

Key Clinical Considerations ▶
  • Developmental surveillance is a longitudinal process — a single screen is a snapshot, not a diagnosis. Flag borderline scores for NP review and schedule follow-up rather than discharging concern.
  • ASQ-3 cut-off scores indicate risk, not delay. A child scoring in the monitoring zone warrants structured follow-up; a child scoring below cut-off with strong parental concern still warrants clinical attention.
  • Growth parameters should always be interpreted in the context of trajectory, not single data points. Faltering growth or unexpected BMI trajectory requires NP review.
  • Vision and hearing concerns identified during CHDC should prompt direct referral — do not wait for the next scheduled check.
  • Document all parental concerns explicitly, even where screening is within normal limits. Parental concern is itself a clinical finding.
Escalate / Refer to NP ▶
  • ASQ-3 score below cut-off in two or more domains
  • Parental or educator concern not explained by screening result
  • Growth faltering, obesity, or microcephaly/macrocephaly
  • Any concern regarding vision, hearing, or neurological signs
  • Child known to child protection or with complex psychosocial history
Core Clinical Guidelines
RCH Clinical Practice Guidelines
Royal Children's Hospital Melbourne. Broad paediatric guidelines — investigations, management pathways, emergency presentations.
Free
Healthy Under 5 Kids
NT Health child health education resource covering development, assessment, perinatal mental health, and Aboriginal child-rearing practices.

SA-specific clinical pathways, child protection guidelines, and statewide policies.
FreeSA

Evidence-based clinical guidelines across paediatrics, antibiotics, respiratory, and more.

Science of early childhood development — toxic stress, resilience, serve-and-return interaction, and policy implications. Strong evidence synthesis.
Free
Sleep
Red Nose — Safe Sleep
Australian safe sleep guidelines for infants — safe sleeping environments, SUDI risk reduction, and parent education resources.

BASIS — Baby Sleep Information Source
UK-based evidence-based infant sleep resource. Normal infant sleep biology, breastfeeding and sleep, and evidence summaries for clinicians and parents.

FreeSA
CFCA — Child Family Community Australia

Section 02
Perinatal & Early Parenting
Scope▶

This section covers the perinatal period through the first two years of life, including antenatal context, postnatal adaptation, infant feeding, and perinatal mental health. While Little Grove does not deliver antenatal care, nurses frequently encounter families where perinatal factors remain clinically relevant — prematurity, feeding difficulties, perinatal mental health history, and birth trauma all have ongoing developmental implications encountered in the CHDC context.

Key Clinical Considerations▶
  • Perinatal mental health conditions — including depression, anxiety, PTSD, and psychosis — can present or persist well beyond the immediate postnatal period. Do not assume resolution at six weeks or six months.
  • The Edinburgh Postnatal Depression Scale (EPDS) is a screening tool, not a diagnostic instrument. A score ≥13 warrants NP review; a positive response to question 10 (self-harm) requires immediate NP escalation regardless of total score.
  • Corrected age must be used for developmental assessment in preterm infants (born before 37 weeks) until 24 months corrected, and considered up to 36 months for significant prematurity.
  • Fathers, partners, and non-birthing parents are also at risk of perinatal mental health difficulties and should not be overlooked in family assessment.
  • Breastfeeding difficulties are a common presenting concern. Ensure referral pathways to IBCLC support are current and accessible.
Escalate / Refer to NP▶
  • EPDS ≥13, or any score with positive response to Q10
  • Infant feeding concerns not resolving with standard lactation support
  • Maternal or paternal mental health concern at any CHDC encounter
  • Preterm infant with developmental trajectory not consistent with corrected age norms
  • Any disclosure of family violence in the perinatal context
Guidelines & Clinical Reference
RANZCOG Clinical Guidelines
Antenatal care, obstetric complications, postnatal care, and gynaecology guidelines.
Free
SA Perinatal Practice Guidelines
SA Health authoritative perinatal guidelines — the primary SA-specific reference for perinatal clinical practice.
FreeSA
Pregnancy Birth & Baby (Healthdirect)
Clinically reviewed consumer content — useful for anticipatory guidance referrals and parent letters.
Free
Perinatal Mental Health
COPE — Centre of Perinatal Excellence
Australian evidence-based perinatal mental health guidelines, EPDS guidance, clinician tools, and professional development.
Free
PANDA — Health Professionals
Perinatal Anxiety & Depression Australia. Clinician referral pathways, screening resources, and consumer referral support.
Free
Beyond Blue — Perinatal Mental Health
EPDS guidance, perinatal depression and anxiety resources for clinicians and families.
Free
Lactation & Infant Feeding
ACLN — Lactation Resource Centre
Australian Breastfeeding Association clinical resources, lactation references, and professional support.
Free
LactMed (NLM)
Drug and chemical safety in breastfeeding. NLM-maintained, regularly updated evidence summaries.
Free
IBC — Lactation Information Sheets
Infant Breastfeeding Centre of Calgary. Comprehensive, evidence-based lactation information sheets covering nipple pain, supply, tongue tie, jaundice, and more.
Free
Section 03
Asthma, Allergies & Eczema
Scope▶

This section covers the atopic conditions most commonly encountered in the ECEC and CHDC setting — eczema, food allergy, anaphylaxis, and asthma. These conditions frequently co-occur as part of the atopic march and should prompt consideration of the full atopic picture when any one condition is identified. Resources include clinical guidelines, action plan templates, and patient education materials suitable for use in ECEC settings.

Key Clinical Considerations▶
  • Eczema is the most common early atopic presentation and is strongly associated with subsequent food allergy and asthma. Severity and control should be assessed at every CHDC encounter in the first three years.
  • Food allergy confirmation requires formal allergen testing — do not advise elimination diets based on symptom history alone. Refer for formal allergy assessment.
  • Anaphylaxis action plan currency is a patient safety issue in ECEC settings. Confirm that any child with a known allergy has a current, signed ASCIA action plan and that it is accessible to ECEC staff.
  • Asthma diagnosis in children under five is inherently uncertain — the preferred term is episodic wheeze. Avoid labelling unless diagnosis has been formally established by a treating practitioner.
  • Spacer technique is frequently incorrect. Brief education at the point of care significantly improves inhaled medication delivery.
Escalate / Refer to NP▶
  • Suspected new food allergy or anaphylaxis history without prior formal assessment
  • Eczema not controlled on standard emollient and low-potency topical corticosteroid therapy
  • Recurrent wheeze or parental concern regarding asthma diagnosis or management
  • Out-of-date or absent anaphylaxis action plan — do not discharge without a management plan in place
  • Any child presenting with an acute allergic reaction requiring clinical assessment
Asthma
Australian Asthma Handbook
National Asthma Council. Evidence-based guidelines for diagnosis, stepwise management, and action plans.
Free
NAC — Asthma Action Plans
Printable action plans, spacer technique resources, and patient education materials.
FreePrintable
Allergy & Anaphylaxis
ASCIA — Clinical Resources
Australasian Society of Clinical Immunology & Allergy. Guidelines, action plans, food allergy, anaphylaxis, and immunodeficiency.
Free
ASCIA — Anaphylaxis Action Plans
Printable action plans for anaphylaxis and allergic reactions — EpiPen and Anapen versions.
FreePrintable
Allergy & Anaphylaxis Australia
Family resources, ELMO e-learning for educators, and clinical support materials.
Free
Eczema & Dermatology
DermNet NZ
Comprehensive dermatology reference — excellent image library, condition-specific management, differential diagnosis support.
Free
ASCIA — Eczema / Atopic Dermatitis
Atopic march, stepwise management, wet dressings, and topical corticosteroid guidance.
Free
Eczema Support Australia
Patient education resources, steroid phobia tools, and clinical support materials.
Free
Section 04
Mental Health & Behaviour
Scope▶

This section covers child and adolescent mental health, neurodevelopmental conditions, and behaviour concerns as they present in the CHDC and clinic context. It includes resources for screening, referral, and parent support across ADHD, autism spectrum, anxiety, trauma, and general behavioural concerns. Parenting support and behaviour resources are included here, reflecting the evidence that parent-child interaction is the primary lever for early childhood mental health intervention.

Key Clinical Considerations▶
  • Behavioural concerns in young children are rarely categorical — resist premature diagnostic framing and focus on functional impact, developmental context, and family capacity.
  • ADHD and autism frequently co-occur and share symptom overlap. Screening tools such as SNAP-IV and SDQ inform but do not diagnose — formal assessment requires specialist referral.
  • Trauma exposure — including domestic violence, neglect, and adverse childhood experiences (ACEs) — is highly prevalent in the populations Little Grove serves and commonly presents as behavioural dysregulation rather than identifiable psychological symptoms.
  • Parental mental health is the strongest predictor of child mental health outcomes. Assessment of the child always includes assessment of the caregiving environment.
  • Behaviour management strategies should be evidence-based. Triple P and other structured parenting programs have strong evidence in the 2–12 year age range.
Escalate / Refer to NP▶
  • SDQ total difficulties score in the abnormal range (≥17 parent-report)
  • SNAP-IV scores suggesting ADHD requiring formal assessment
  • Any disclosure or clinical suspicion of trauma, abuse, or neglect — follow child protection pathway
  • Parental mental health concern identified in the context of child behavioural presentation
  • Suicidal ideation, self-harm, or acute psychological disturbance at any age
Child & Youth Mental Health
headspace — Clinical Resources
Youth mental health clinical tools, referral pathways, and resources for 12–25 year olds.
Free
Orygen — Youth Mental Health
Early psychosis clinical guidelines, youth mental health resources, and clinician training tools.
Free

Free

AADPA — Australian ADHD Clinical Practice Guideline
2022 NHMRC-endorsed guideline. 111 recommendations across diagnosis, assessment, and treatment of ADHD across the lifespan. Free download.
Free
AADPA — ADHD Prescribing Guide eBook
RACGP-endorsed practical prescribing reference for Australian clinicians. Medication choice, dosing, monitoring, adverse effects, and specific populations including paediatrics.
PurchaseNP
Autism CRC
Australian autism research and clinical resources, diagnosis pathways, and evidence summaries.
Free
Novopsych — Assessment Library
Australian digital assessment platform. ADHD, autism, anxiety, and developmental screening tools — administered and scored online. Includes SNAP-IV, SDQ, and other validated instruments.
Subscription

Modified Checklist for Autism in Toddlers, Revised with Follow-Up. Free, validated 2-stage autism screening tool for 16–48 months. Download and scoring instructions at mchatscreen.com.

Parenting & Behaviour Support
Raising Children Network
Evidence-based parenting resource from newborn to teens. Excellent for anticipatory guidance and parent letter referrals.
Free
Triple P — Positive Parenting Program
Tiered parenting support program. Clinician information, referral resources, and practitioner training.

SA child and family health service — parent resources, sleep, behaviour, and feeding. SA-specific referral context.

Section 05
Infectious Disease & Immunisation
Scope▶

This section covers immunisation practice, communicable disease management, and antimicrobial guidance as relevant to child and adolescent primary care. In the ECEC context, nurses are frequently required to advise on exclusion periods, outbreak management, and immunisation status. Resources include the Australian Immunisation Handbook, ATAGI updates, SA Health communicable disease guidance, and antimicrobial references for NP prescribing.

Key Clinical Considerations▶
  • Immunisation status should be confirmed at every CHDC encounter using the Australian Immunisation Register (AIR). Do not rely on parent-held records as the sole source of truth.
  • Exclusion periods for common childhood infections are governed by SA Health communicable disease guidelines — these differ in some instances from national guidance and should be the reference used for ECEC advice.
  • Catch-up schedules for partially immunised children can be complex. The AIR and the Australian Immunisation Handbook catch-up calculator are the primary references — escalate to NP for children with significant gaps or complex history.
  • Empirical antibiotic selection should always reference current eTG Antibiotic guidelines. Local resistance patterns matter — do not rely on historical prescribing habits.
  • Sepsis in children can be rapidly progressive and is easily underestimated. Any child with fever and clinical deterioration, particularly under 3 months of age, requires urgent escalation.
Escalate / Refer to NP▶
  • Complex or incomplete immunisation history requiring individualised catch-up planning
  • Suspected vaccine-preventable disease — notifiable disease obligations apply
  • Child under 3 months with fever, regardless of apparent clinical stability
  • Any child with signs of systemic infection, sepsis, or meningism
  • Antimicrobial prescribing decisions — outside RN scope
Immunisation
Australian Immunisation Handbook
Authoritative national reference — schedules, catch-up, contraindications, and adverse event management.
Free
ATAGI Statements
Advisory Technical Advisory Group on Immunisation. Updated guidance on new vaccines and schedule changes.
Free
NCIRS
National Centre for Immunisation Research. Vaccine safety evidence summaries and clinical support resources.
Free
Antimicrobial Guidelines
eTG — Antibiotic Guidelines
First-line empirical antibiotic choices for paediatric and adult presentations. Primary prescribing reference.
Institutional login

FreeSA
You've Got What? — SA Health
SA Health fact sheets on common infectious diseases — spread, symptoms, prevention, and when to seek care. Useful for parent communication and ECEC advice.
FreeSA
Exclusion Periods — Childcare, Preschool & School
SA Health recommended minimum exclusion periods for infectious diseases in childcare and education settings. Primary reference for ECEC exclusion advice.
FreeSA
RCH — Sepsis Recognition & Management
Paediatric sepsis pathway — recognition, resuscitation, investigation, and empirical antibiotic guidance.
Free
CDC Yellow Book — Travel Health
International travel health guidance. Useful for regional and newly arrived families with recent travel history.
Free
Section 06
Sexual & Reproductive Health
Scope▶

This section covers sexual and reproductive health as encountered in the adolescent primary care context, including STI screening and management, contraception, and reproductive health counselling. In the Little Grove context, SRH is most relevant to older adolescents presenting through the clinic. SA-specific services — including SHINE SA — are the primary referral pathway. Confidentiality, consent, and mandatory reporting obligations require particular attention in this population.

Key Clinical Considerations▶
  • Adolescent confidentiality is a legal and ethical cornerstone of SRH practice. Familiarise yourself with Gillick competence and the South Australian legal framework for consent in minors before engaging in SRH consultations.
  • A sexually active adolescent under 16 requires careful assessment of the relationship context. Age-of-consent legislation and mandatory reporting obligations must be applied — escalate to NP if uncertain.
  • Chlamydia is the most common notifiable STI in South Australian adolescents and is frequently asymptomatic. Opportunistic screening should be considered in sexually active patients.
  • Emergency contraception is time-critical. Know your referral pathway and do not delay — SHINE SA provides same-day access.
  • LGBTQ+ adolescents have elevated rates of mental health difficulties, SRH risk, and healthcare avoidance. Ensure clinical interactions are inclusive and non-assumptive.
Escalate / Refer to NP▶
  • Any SRH consultation involving a patient under 16 — mandatory reporting assessment required
  • STI diagnosis or treatment — prescribing is within NP scope only
  • Suspected sexual assault or coercion at any age — child protection and forensic pathways apply
  • Complex contraception including LARC (IUD, implant) — requires specialist referral
  • Adolescent disclosing pregnancy
SA Services & Pathways
SHINE SA — Health Professionals
SA sexual health service. STI management, contraception resources, and specialist referral pathways.
FreeSA
STI Guidelines & Management
STIGMA — National STI Guidelines
ASHM-produced national STI management guidelines — testing, treatment, and partner notification.
Free
Contraception & Adolescent SRH
In the Know — SRH Factsheets
Family Planning Australia's clinical factsheet library covering contraception, STIs, sexual health, and reproductive health for clinicians.
Free
Section 07
Women's Health
Scope▶

This section covers women's health conditions commonly encountered in the Little Grove clinical context, including polycystic ovary syndrome, endometriosis, and gynaecological presentations. While Little Grove does not deliver primary gynaecological care, NPs and nurses frequently encounter women with these conditions alongside their child health presentations, and clinicians should be equipped to recognise, support, and refer appropriately. Resources are relevant across the lifespan from adolescence through to perimenopause.

Key Clinical Considerations▶
  • PCOS is the most common endocrine condition in women of reproductive age and is frequently underdiagnosed. Adolescent presentation overlaps with normal pubertal changes — apply the 2023 international evidence-based guideline diagnostic criteria carefully in this age group.
  • Endometriosis has an average diagnostic delay of 6.5–8 years in Australia. Dysmenorrhoea presenting in adolescence warrants clinical attention rather than dismissal as normal.
  • Women's health conditions frequently co-present with mental health comorbidities — anxiety, depression, and disordered eating are significantly elevated in PCOS and endometriosis populations.
  • Many women presenting in a child health context are themselves in perimenopause or managing chronic gynaecological conditions. A brief systems review supports whole-family care.
  • Adolescent girls with irregular cycles, hirsutism, acne, or weight concerns warrant consideration of PCOS and appropriate investigation before attributing symptoms to puberty alone.
Escalate / Refer to NP or Specialist▶
  • Suspected PCOS in adolescence — formal investigation and management planning required
  • Dysmenorrhoea not responding to first-line analgesia — endometriosis workup warranted
  • Abnormal uterine bleeding in adolescents — rule out coagulopathy and structural causes
  • Any gynaecological presentation with significant mental health comorbidity
  • Perimenopausal symptoms significantly impacting quality of life — MHT consideration
PCOS
Monash PCOS — International Evidence-Based Guideline
2023 internationally endorsed PCOS clinical practice guideline. Diagnosis, lifestyle, psychological, and medical management across the lifespan including adolescence.
Free
Jean Hailes — PCOS Clinical Resources
Australian women's health organisation. PCOS clinical tools, patient resources, and management guidance aligned with the Monash guideline.
Free
Endometriosis
Endometriosis Australia
Clinical resources, patient education, and referral pathways for endometriosis. Includes resources for adolescent presentation and GP/NP management.
Free
Jean Hailes — Endometriosis
Evidence-based endometriosis clinical and patient resources. Diagnostic pathway, management options, and pain management guidance.
Free
General Women's Health Guidelines
The Women's — Clinical Guidelines for GPs
Royal Women's Hospital Melbourne. Clinical guidelines covering gynaecology, obstetrics, and women's health for primary care practitioners.
Free
Jean Hailes — Health Professionals Hub
Australian evidence-based women's health clinical resources across menstrual health, menopause, pelvic floor, and mental health.
Free
Australasian Menopause Society
Clinical information sheets on menopause, HRT/MHT, perimenopause, and related conditions. Relevant for mothers presenting alongside child health consultations.
Free
RANZCOG — Women's Health Guidelines
Authoritative Australian clinical guidelines across obstetrics, gynaecology, and women's health. Cross-reference with Perinatal section for antenatal and postnatal guidance.
Free
Section 08
Aboriginal Health
Scope▶

This section covers clinical resources, cultural frameworks, and population health data relevant to Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander children and families across Little Grove's service areas — metropolitan Adelaide, the Fleurieu Peninsula, Murray-Mallee, and Kangaroo Island. Resources are provided to support clinical practice, but cultural safety is a relational competency that cannot be resourced alone — ongoing engagement with Aboriginal communities and ACCHOs in our service areas is essential.

Key Clinical Considerations▶
  • The health gap between Aboriginal and non-Aboriginal Australians is not explained by biology — it is the product of historical dispossession, ongoing structural racism, and social determinants of health. Clinical interactions should reflect this understanding.
  • Developmental screening tools including the ASQ-3 were normed on predominantly white Western populations. Scores should be interpreted with cultural and contextual awareness, and parental knowledge of their child's capabilities weighted accordingly.
  • Family and community structures in Aboriginal contexts may differ significantly from the nuclear family model assumed by many clinical tools. Identify the child's primary carers carefully and without assumption.
  • Hearing loss — including otitis media with effusion — is disproportionately prevalent in Aboriginal children and has significant developmental and educational consequences. Maintain a lower threshold for audiological referral.
  • Mandatory reporting obligations apply equally to all children. However, the history of Aboriginal children being removed from families demands that child protection decisions are made with particular care, cultural consultation, and awareness of systemic bias.
Escalate / Refer to NP▶
  • Any clinical concern where cultural safety or appropriate engagement is uncertain — seek Aboriginal health worker or ACCHO input first
  • Hearing concerns in Aboriginal children — lower threshold for referral than general population
  • Complex developmental presentation in a child with significant social adversity
  • Any child protection concern — follow standard pathway with cultural consultation where possible
Clinical Guidelines
CARPA Standard Treatment Manual — 8th Edition
Central Australian Rural Practitioners Association. Practical primary care reference with strong regional and remote focus. Direct PDF download.
FreePDF
Healthy Under 5 Kids — Education Package
NT Dept of Health & CDU. 8-module clinician education package covering child development, assessment, perinatal MH, and Aboriginal child-rearing practices. 2009 — review for currency.
FreePDF
MBS Billing Rules — Aboriginal & TSI Health Care
Services Australia. MBS billing rules and requirements for Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander primary health care, including 715 Health Assessment items.
Free
RACGP National Guide — Aboriginal Health
Evidence-based preventive health guidance for Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander people in primary care.
Free
Cultural Safety & Population Health
SA Health — Aboriginal Health
SA Aboriginal health strategy, cultural safety frameworks, and SA ACCHO contacts.
FreeSA
Lowitja Institute
Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander health research. Evidence base for culturally safe practice and health equity.
Free
AIHW — Indigenous Health Data
Closing the Gap data, health outcome indicators, and population health data for Aboriginal and TSI communities.
Free
Section 09
CALD Resources
Scope▶

This section provides resources to support clinically and culturally safe practice with children and families from culturally and linguistically diverse backgrounds. Little Grove's service areas include significant CALD communities, particularly in metropolitan Adelaide. Resources include translated health materials, interpreter access, and frameworks for culturally responsive practice. CALD is not a clinical category — it describes a heterogeneous group of communities, each with distinct health beliefs, language needs, and migration histories.

Key Clinical Considerations▶
  • Always offer a professional interpreter for families with limited English proficiency. Using children or family members as interpreters is inappropriate in clinical settings and may compromise informed consent, mandatory reporting, and clinical accuracy.
  • TIS National (131 450) provides free telephone interpreter services for healthcare providers, available 24 hours. This should be the default, not a last resort.
  • Refugee and humanitarian entrant families may have significant trauma histories, interrupted healthcare, incomplete immunisation records, and undiagnosed health conditions. A lower threshold for comprehensive assessment is appropriate.
  • Health literacy varies independently of English language proficiency. Confirmed understanding — not just nodded agreement — is part of informed consent.
  • Cultural health beliefs are not barriers to be overcome — they are part of the clinical picture. Explore rather than dismiss.
Escalate / Refer to NP▶
  • Complex health history in a newly arrived refugee or humanitarian entrant family
  • Immunisation records absent or unverifiable — catch-up assessment required
  • Any safeguarding concern where language or cultural barriers are affecting assessment — do not proceed without interpreter support
  • Mental health presentations in CALD families — higher risk of misattribution without cultural context
Translated Health Resources
Health Translations — Victoria
Largest Australian repository of translated health resources. Child health, immunisation, mental health in 100+ languages.
Free
Raising Children — Other Languages
Key parenting and child development resources available in Arabic, Chinese, Vietnamese, and other languages.
Free
Interpreter & Communication Services
TIS National — Translating & Interpreting
Free telephone interpreter service for healthcare providers — 24/7 in 160+ languages. Call 131 450.
Free to providers
Can Do 4 Kids — SA
SA service supporting children and young people who are deaf or hard of hearing. Auslan interpreting, early intervention, and family support.
FreeSA
Braille Australia — Resources
Resources supporting children and families with vision impairment. Braille literacy, tactile learning, and accessible health information.
Free
Practice Frameworks
Orygen — Responding Together
Orygen policy resource on culturally responsive youth mental health care. Frameworks for working with young people from CALD backgrounds.
Free
Section 10 · NP Access Only
NP Clinical Practice
Scope▶

This section is intended for Nurse Practitioner use and covers prescribing references, clinical decision support tools, Medicare and PBS requirements, and AHPRA regulatory resources relevant to NP practice at Little Grove. Prescribing decisions must always be made within the individual NP's endorsement schedule and collaborative arrangement. When in doubt regarding scope, consult your endorsement documentation before prescribing.

Key Clinical Considerations▶
  • PBS authority prescribing requirements for NPs differ from those for medical practitioners in several important respects. Always verify current PBS schedule listings for NP-specific restrictions before prescribing, particularly for stimulant medications, guanfacine, and atypical antipsychotics.
  • Weight-based dosing in paediatrics requires a current reference — use PBMG as the primary paediatric dosing reference and cross-check with AMH for interactions and contraindications.
  • Prescribing in lactation requires specific risk assessment — LactMed is the most current and evidence-graded free reference.
  • eTG Antibiotic guidelines represent current best-practice empirical therapy. Departures from first-line recommendations should be documented with clinical rationale.
  • MBS item 82200 series NP items have specific eligibility requirements including MyMedicare registration implications — ensure billing is accurate and defensible.
Escalate / Consult / Refer to Specialist▶
  • Prescribing outside your individual endorsement schedule — do not proceed
  • Paediatric presentations requiring specialist input prior to initiating treatment (e.g. new stimulant initiation, first antipsychotic prescription)
  • Any adverse drug reaction — document, report via TGA ADRAC, and review ongoing management
  • Clinical presentations beyond NP scope — maintain current and transparent collaborative arrangements
Prescribing & Medicines References
Therapeutic Guidelines (eTG Complete)
Evidence-based guidelines across paediatrics, antibiotic, respiratory, analgesic, and neurology. Primary prescribing reference.
Institutional loginNP + RN
Australian Medicines Handbook (AMH)
Comprehensive medicines reference — dosing, interactions, pregnancy/lactation safety, and adverse effects.
🔗 Add URLNP
Paediatric & Baby Medicines Guide (PBMG)
Paediatric-specific dosing reference. Essential for weight-based dosing in child and adolescent prescribing.
FreeNP
PBS Schedule
Current PBS listings, authority prescribing requirements, streamlined authorities, and NP-specific restrictions.
FreeNP
TGA — ARTG Search
Australian Register of Therapeutic Goods. Approved indications, consumer and product information documents.
FreeNP

Evidence-graded clinical decision support with strong paediatric DDx tool. Diagnosis, investigation, and management pathways. Widely used in Australian clinical settings.
SubscriptionNP

FreeNP

Free online radiology tutorials. Chest X-ray anatomy, interpretation, and pathology — practical reference for NP chest assessment and report interpretation.

Evidence-based clinical decision support with graded recommendations. Strong paediatrics, pharmacology, and DDx content.

FreeNP

Free with account. Drug interaction checker (up to 30 drugs simultaneously), 129 clinical calculators, disease monographs, and CME. Widely used by Australian NPs for point-of-care prescribing support.
FreeNP
Life in the Fast Lane (LITFL)
Australian-produced emergency medicine reference. Exceptional free ECG library, QTc and drug toxicity interpretation, and clinical toxicology — directly relevant to stimulant and antipsychotic prescribing.
FreeNP
Drugs.com — Interaction Checker
Free drug interaction checker with high sensitivity. Use as a second-line check for complex medication combinations including stimulants, antipsychotics, and guanfacine.
FreeNP
MBS & Regulatory
MBS Online
Medicare item descriptors. NP-specific item numbers (82200 series), telehealth eligibility, and billing guidance.
FreeNP
NMBA — NP Standards for Practice
Regulatory standards governing NP scope, prescribing authority, and collaborative practice arrangements.
FreeNP
AHPRA
Registration status, CPD requirements, notifications, and prescribing endorsement verification.
FreeNP
